posts.text: As American images went dark, Chinese alternatives filled the void. It marks the end of a Western monopoly on advanced visuals. But register for free to learn why, for open-source analysts, it is also a warning https://www.economist.com/china/2026/04/19/how-chinese-satellites-have-boosted-irans-war-effort?taid=69e656e41677ba0001448711&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter
posts.author: TheEconomist

posts.text:
Lastly, our view of "own assets or be left behind" is gaining popularity.

This is exactly why the S&P 500 just added +$7.3 TRILLION in 14 trading days.

The gap in the K-shaped US economy is only widening.

Follow us @KobeissiLetter for real time analysis as this develops.
posts.author: KobeissiLetter

posts.text:
@BambroughKevin posted April 17 about the importance of percolation thresholds in fully understanding the magic of $HGRAF's Turbostratic Fractal Graphene (FGA-1 Fractal Graphene™). https://x.com/BambroughKevin/status/2045171434192838975

I decided to investigate via an @grok query. The query and response are posted below. Certainly supports Kevin's faith in the specialness of Hydrograph--and ours, too, if--like me--you're an $HGRAF investor.
Can you examine percolation thresholds for turbostratic fractal graphene, and also explain what advantages the attributes of Hydrograph graphene's percolation threshold's offer to companies seeking to add graphene to their product lines?

In short, HydroGraph’s turbostratic fractal graphene lowers the barrier to graphene adoption by delivering outsized performance at loadings that are practically and economically viable—allowing companies to enhance products across batteries, composites, construction, electronics, and coatings without compromising processability, weight, or cost structures. This is why R&D teams highlight its percolation behavior as a game-changer. Specific thresholds in your matrix would require targeted testing, but the material’s attributes reliably enable ultra-low thresholds.

Percolation threshold refers to the critical filler concentration (typically in vol% or wt%) in a composite (e.g., polymer, battery electrode, coating, or cement) at which a continuous conductive network forms, causing a sharp rise in electrical conductivity (or other transport properties). Below this threshold, the material is insulating or poorly conductive; above it, conductivity jumps dramatically as pathways connect. For graphene-based fillers, the threshold depends heavily on the graphene’s morphology, dispersion quality, aspect ratio, and tendency to aggregate/restack fractal graphene (specifically HydroGraph Clean Power’s FGA-1 Fractal Graphene™) is a proprietary few-layer graphene produced via detonation (explosion) synthesis of carbon-bearing gases. Key structural attributes include:Turbostratic stacking: Layers (average ~6 layers, range 3–9 or ≤10) are rotationally misaligned (not AB-stacked like graphite), leading to an interlayer spacing of ~0.34–0.35 nm. This prevents easy restacking into inert graphite during processing/dispersion.

Fractal aggregate morphology: Nano-platelets (lateral size 20–50 nm, aspect ratio ~1:15, thickness ~3 ± 0.5 nm) self-assemble into branched, self-similar fractal aggregates (radius of gyration ~150 nm). This creates a high effective volume fraction and maximizes inter-particle contacts.

Pristine properties: 100% sp² bonding, 99.8% carbon purity, 0.2% oxygen, no impurities/PAHs/functionalization detected, D/G ratio ~0.68 (Raman), SSA 130–200 m²/g, zeta potential +13 to +60 mV (aids dispersion), and powder conductivity 100–300 S/m (compression-dependent). 

These features enable exceptionally low percolation thresholds compared to conventional graphene, carbon black, CNTs, or graphite. The fractal geometry and turbostratic nature promote efficient 3D networking and uniform dispersion at minimal loadings, while the high aspect ratio and edge-site density further reduce the filler needed for connectivity. Exact thresholds are matrix- and processing-dependent (and not always publicly quantified in absolute terms), but applications and collaborations demonstrate ultra-low effective values:

In battery electrode slurries (via NEI Corp. dispersions): Explicitly noted for a “lower percolation threshold” vs. spherical carbon black due to 2D platelet interconnectivity, improving rate capability and energy density at low additive levels. http://neicorporation.com

In highly filled polymer composites (e.g., vinyl ester for bipolar plates): Just 0.09 wt% fractal graphene (on top of ~50 wt% total carbon) significantly boosts in-plane/through-plane conductivity; 0.6 wt% alone yields ~82% in-plane and ~133% through-plane gains vs. baseline. http://uwo.scholaris.ca

Broader examples: Conductive networks form at <1 wt% in coatings/ESD applications (vs. 5–15% for traditional fibers); 0.02 wt% in cementitious binders for major strength gains; 10–100× performance uplift vs. conventional graphene at ultra-low loadings across plastics, coatings, and supercapacitors. http://hydrograph.com 

General graphene/polymer thresholds can be as low as ~0.3 vol% in optimized cases, but HydroGraph’s material consistently outperforms due to its non-agglomerating, high-contact fractal design—often described as “blowing R&D teams’ minds” for enabling conductivity/mechanical enhancements far below typical loadings.Advantages of these low-percolation attributes for companies adding graphene to product lines are substantial and directly tied to the material’s structure:

Cost and material efficiency: Graphene is expensive; achieving target conductivity, strength, or multifunctionality at 0.02–1 wt% (vs. several % for alternatives) drastically cuts filler usage, raw-material costs, and overall formulation expense while enabling lighter-weight end products. http://hydrograph.com

Preservation of base-matrix properties: Low loadings minimize viscosity increases, maintain flexibility/elongation/transparency (where relevant), and avoid processing issues common with high-fillers. This is critical for coatings, flexible electronics, plastics, and composites. http://neicorporation.com

Multifunctionality at minimal addition: The same low dose often delivers simultaneous electrical conductivity, mechanical reinforcement (tensile/flexural strength/modulus), thermal management, EMI shielding (e.g., 80 dB at <1 mm thickness), barrier properties, and more—unlocking performance without trade-offs. http://hydrograph.com

Application-specific gains:

Batteries/supercapacitors: Better active-material interconnectivity → higher energy density, faster charging, and easier integration into existing slurries (water- or NMP-based dispersions available).neicorporation.comPolymers/plastics: 

Lightweighting, conductive plastics, or enhanced composites at industrial scale with consistent batch-to-batch quality.

Cement/concrete: 0.02 wt% boosts early strength up to 21% and enables higher SCM use (reducing cement by ~17%), cutting CO₂ emissions. http://hydrograph.com

Coatings/ESD/EMI: Permanent conductivity without migration or high filler penalties.

Supply-chain and sustainability edge: 
Pristine, scalable U.S. production (patented detonation process, modular Hyperion systems, tonnage capacity), regulatory clearances (EPA TSCA, REACH), zero solvents/catalysts, and lowest environmental footprint make adoption reliable and ESG-friendly. Identical batches eliminate variability that plagues other graphenes. http://hydrograph.com
posts.author: BambroughKevin
